challenges.title: Call Counter Decorator
challenges.description: Write a decorator `count_calls` that tracks how many times a decorated function is called. Store the count in a `.calls` attribute on the wrapper function. Use functools.wraps to preserve the original function's name.
challenges.starter_code:
import functools

def count_calls(func):
    """Decorator that counts how many times a function is called."""
    # Your code here
    pass

@count_calls
def say_hello(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

# Test it
print(say_hello("Alice"))
print(say_hello("Bob"))
print(f"Called {say_hello.calls} times")
challenges.test_code:
assert say_hello("Test") == "Hello, Test!"
assert say_hello.calls >= 1
prev = say_hello.calls
say_hello("Again")
assert say_hello.calls == prev + 1
assert say_hello.__name__ == "say_hello"
hints: Define a wrapper function inside count_calls
Use a mutable container (list) or setattr to track count

challenges.title: Retry Decorator
challenges.description: Write a decorator factory `retry(max_attempts=3)` that retries the decorated function up to max_attempts times if it raises an exception. If all attempts fail, re-raise the last exception. Return the result on success.
challenges.starter_code:
def retry(max_attempts=3):
    """Decorator factory that retries a function on failure."""
    def decorator(func):
        # Your code here
        pass
    return decorator

# Test it
attempt_count = 0

@retry(max_attempts=3)
def flaky_function():
    global attempt_count
    attempt_count += 1
    if attempt_count < 3:
        raise ValueError("Not ready yet")
    return "success"

result = flaky_function()
print(f"Result: {result}, took {attempt_count} attempts")
hints: retry() returns a decorator, which returns a wrapper — three nested functions
Use a for loop with range(max_attempts) inside the wrapper
Catch Exception in the loop, re-raise on the last attempt
